summary: This iteration derives a closed-form predictor for the optimal MaxBatchSize (M-hat) of a vLLM queue and validates its load-bearing assumptions purely against the analytic formulas (formulas.py / baseline.json) — with NO oracle (/target) calls, NO truth-cache reads, and NO code changes. The central claim is that when the chunk count nc(B)=1 across the batch range [1,256], the inter-token-latency (ITL) curve is exactly affine, so a simple floor-based formula matches an exhaustive brute-force scan with zero error.
key_points: WHAT & WHY: Replace brute-force search for the best MaxBatchSize with an exact closed form. The mechanism: with nc=1, the per-step increment delta is independent of B (formulas.py:69-73), making itl(B) affine, so inverting it for an integer threshold is exact.

h-main (core claim): Across all six dev scenarios, the formula M_ITL = clamp(floor(1 + (targetITL - itl(1))/delta), 1, 256) must equal the brute-scan largest-feasible B with ZERO discrepancy, and itl(B), ttft_prefill(B), tau(B) must all be strictly increasing. Note: exactness is claimed against the brute scan of the same primitive, NOT against the oracle.

h-control-negative (specificity): For the 'unbounded' scenario, itl(256)=15.94 < targetITL=200, so no interior ITL crossing exists and the formula must return M_max=256 — confirming the predicted cap only appears when a constraint actually binds.

h-ablation (necessity of nc=1): On a synthetic high-input profile (7500 input tokens, 64 output, targetITL=200) nc rises to 2 within range, breaking affineness; the single-slope formula should mispredict by one step (predicts 30 vs true 31), proving nc=1 is a necessary precondition and scoping the high-input/benchmark regime as 'needs piecewise analysis.'

h-robustness (why latency, not saturation, sets M*): Saturation throughput S(B)=1000*B/tau(B) should be monotone increasing and concave, with S(256) below the analytic ceiling S_inf (ratio 0.75-0.96, approaching from below), meaning throughput never decreases in M — so the binding limit comes from latency constraints, not saturation.

CONFIRM if: zero formula-vs-scan discrepancy on all six scenarios, monotone primitives, unbounded returns 256, ablation breaks exactness by ~1 step, and S(B) stays monotone/concave below ceiling. REFUTE if any scenario shows discrepancy (check whether nc jumped within range or bg hit its zero floor), or if S(B) is non-monotone or exceeds the ceiling.

SCOPE/RISK: Low risk — observation-only iteration with no code changes and no oracle dependence. Main limitation is that exactness is validated against the analytic primitive, not against the true /target oracle, so directional-only claims (M_ITL as a lower bound on the oracle plateau onset) remain untested until a later iteration.
